title: Why I write for middle grade readers
preview: There's a particular kind of reader — the one who stays up past bedtime with a torch under the covers. I'm writing for them.
content:
There's a particular kind of reader — the one who stays up past bedtime with a torch under the covers. The one who finishes a book and immediately flips back to page one because they're not ready to leave yet.

I was that reader. And I've spent years in classrooms watching those readers discover themselves in the pages of a book. That moment — when a child finds a story that feels like it was written just for them — is one of the most quietly extraordinary things I've ever witnessed.

Middle grade is a specific kind of magic. It's the age when readers are old enough to grapple with real ideas — loss, identity, belonging, courage — but young enough that the world still feels genuinely wondrous. The best middle grade doesn't talk down to its readers. It meets them exactly where they are.

That's what I'm trying to do with the Seasons series. Stories with real stakes, real friendships, and a world that feels worth exploring.

title: Writing a novel series while teaching full time
preview: Term breaks, early mornings, and learning to write in the small pockets of time between everything else.
content:
The honest answer to 'when do you find time to write?' is: I steal it.

I write in the forty minutes before school when the house is still quiet. I write during lunch breaks when the staffroom is empty. I write in the school holidays with the particular intensity of someone who knows the term is coming back.

Teaching is a full-body occupation. It takes your energy, your creativity, your emotional reserves. On the days I come home genuinely depleted, I don't write. I've learned to stop feeling guilty about that.

What I've found is that consistency matters more than volume. Three hundred words on a Tuesday morning is worth more than a thousand words written in a guilt-fuelled burst on a Sunday. The story stays alive when you visit it regularly, even briefly.

The Seasons series exists because I kept returning to it — in the margins, in the small hours, in the gaps between everything else.

title: Middle grade novels every adult should read
preview: The books that shaped me as a reader, and the recent ones reminding me why this genre matters so much.
content:
There's a persistent idea that children's books are somehow lesser — simpler, easier, less worthy of serious attention. I've never believed that, and the books on this list are my evidence.

Middle grade fiction at its best is economical, precise, and emotionally honest in ways that adult literary fiction sometimes isn't brave enough to be. It doesn't have space for self-indulgence. Every sentence has to earn its place.

Some books I return to regularly: A Wrinkle in Time by Madeleine L'Engle, which handles physics and love and courage in the same breath. The Giver by Lois Lowry, which is more unsettling now than when I first read it at eleven. Bridge to Terabithia by Katherine Paterson, which taught me that books are allowed to break your heart.

And more recently: Amari and the Night Brothers by B.B. Alston, which has the best world-building I've encountered in years. The Season of Styx Malone by Kekla Magoon, which is about friendship and summers and the particular ache of growing up.

Read them. Don't let anyone tell you they're not for you.
